data.title: Approaching Nvidia In Stoic Calm: Fourth Valuation Update
data.description:
As someone interested in technology but whose area of expertise does not lie in a related field, I initiated my Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) coverage in June 2024 by soberly focusing on observable financial data and implied market expectations. Sticking to our area of expertise, this is what most of us financial analysts can sensibly do. With this approach, I refrained from judging Wall Street analysts' estimates. Instead, the idea was to do nothing more than derive what markets are pricing into Nvidia.

As a result, it appeared that the “Best Case” was priced into the stock, as I had also titled the first article. Following research pieces of mine kept calling for the “Double-Digit” range as opening potentially fair buying opportunities into Nvidia.

Now, with markets tanking, we have finally reached double-digits. Let us therefore not turn into panic mode and assess market expectations for Nvidia and its valuation considerations equally soberly to find out whether buying at this double-digit price already appears rational in the current environment.

The Beauty Of Valuation

Valuation is and always has been important. When valuation is thrown overboard, exuberance emerges, and that is what has partly happened in recent years.

Applied discount rates have a significant impact on valuation results and should not be based on gut feeling. Although for subjective valuation every investor is free to choose a discount rate that suits him or her, I find it easier to approximate an objectively observable company value, by deriving fair discount rates based on current alternatives and inherent risks that are statistically quantifiable.

Therefore, I continue my cost of capital calculation from last year. See below, how parameters have changed since November 2024. The two most notable changes occurred in (1) the equity risk premium and in (2) Nvidia’s company beta:

  1. Having landed at historically low levels beforehand, equity risk premia have finally increased again, meaning that investors are now more aware of the uncertainties ahead and want to see them priced into current prices, aka want to see them discounted.
  2. Nvidia’s raw beta factors have increased significantly to 2.46 over a 24 months regression period and 1.96 over a 60 months regression period, resulting in a Blume adjusted average of 1.81. This means a significantly elevated beta factor.

All together brings my WACC up by one percentage point to 13%.

Due to Nvidia’s unchanged high Return on Assets and reinvestment behavior, the perpetual growth rate base assumption of 5% remains unchanged.

Proceeding with EPS and cashflow projections, the consensus estimates have shifted upwards only slightly by roughly single digit percentages since November.

Net cash has increased, also slightly bolstering value, yet almost negligible.

Summing up, the significantly increased discount rate mitigates all aforementioned positives. Altogether, the model lands at a fair value range that remains almost completely unchanged between 60 and 70 USD per share. The range is based on worst to best analysts’ estimates.

However, let us apply sensitivities to highly important input parameters such as WACC and terminal growth rate. This shows that at $98, we still stand above the strong green inner range of reasonable valuations, but we approached this area significantly when we saw prices below $90 during Monday’s market opening. In fact, this brought the share price further into the light green widespread sensitivity ranges than it has been since I started covering Nvidia. Nevertheless, do not confuse this with an assessment of a cheap valuation, which is still not evident.

What Everyone Is Talking About – And Some Less Obvious Aspects

There are some talking points that are currently determining the discussion around Nvidia. This chapter aims at briefly addressing them without diving too deep. Because what the completely neutral valuation approach based on publicly available data beforehand did was fusing all these risks and potentially alternating outcomes together and pour it into one number. That was "The Beauty Of Valuation." And these are some narratives behind the numbers:

Concerns surrounding AI-chip overcapacity. Some reports around Microsoft cancelling data center leases fuel these theses. However, an aggregate of reported CAPEX from hyper scalers so far does not indicate a significant slowdown in datacenter investments. Yet, available reports only cover periods including December 2024. In my last coverage, TTM CAPEX of Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon and Tesla totaled $210 billion. Nvidia’s TTM revenue stood at $113 billion. Since then, the aggregate TTM CAPEX of those very same companies have increased by another 12% quarter over quarter, versus Nvidia’s TTM revenue up 15% QoQ.

This seamlessly brings us to revenue recognition and cashflow. As examined in November, Nvidia’s Receivables have recently increased faster than Revenue QoQ. However, Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) are still within historically normal levels, yet show an uptick as well. Free Cashflow, as another indicator, continues to back revenue but also started to grow slower than revenue in the most recent quarter.

The potential effects of tariffs and the ongoing trade disputes in general are thoroughly examined in other articles, and are ideally also implicitly baked into the input parameters of my earlier valuation.

Lastly, Nvidia has been facing an expectations problem for quite some time. Significantly exceeded expectations lead analysts to continuously revise their estimates upwards to a point where we began to see surprises decrease meaningfully. It is not impossible that at some point the increased analysts’ expectations become too hard to beat and will instead be missed.

Takeaway And Personal Involvement Disclaimer

I accumulated Nvidia at two occasions last year with an average share price cost basis between $100 and $110, bringing my position in the red by less than ten percentage points so far. Furthermore, I kept Nvidia’s portfolio weight at a low single-digit percentage. Given the results of this valuation update, I am not willing to double down just yet. Shall we see the base case valuation range in the mid-60s without fundamental deterioration of Nvidia’s business, that would be the next range for larger scale accumulation. Otherwise, I would remain highly content with the currently small position size and enjoy gains on this. Instead, I continue to relatively overweigh the hyper scalers themselves, as they can quickly decide to stop "hyper scaling," leaving cash for themselves and their shareholders instead of paying it to chip manufacturers and designers.

data.title: Rivian: Tesla's Loss Is This Automaker's Gain
data.description:
Sharp fear has overtaken the markets, suddenly sending the major U.S. indices into a correction after a roaring years-long rally. And yet underneath the volatility, investors who are able to stomach some short-term pain have a great opportunity to pick up shares of fantastic growth stocks at a major discount.

Though it's difficult to see this amid the haze of pessimism, a lot of things are going right for Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN), the second-largest EV manufacturer in the U.S. Its main rival, Tesla (TSLA), is facing unprecedented global backlash as a means of protest over Elon Musk's rapid downsizing of the federal government. Meanwhile, new U.S. tariffs are expected to sharply raise the prices of foreign cars, making Rivian's typically higher-end models look more balanced on the pricing spectrum.

Year to date, shares of Rivian have lost ~15% of their value, outpacing losses in the S&P 500. Rivian has held on to its value a bit better than Tesla has this year, of course, but in my view, Rivian has a much better chance of a nearer-term rebound.

I last wrote a bullish note on Rivian in December, when the stock was trading near $14 per share. Since then, Rivian has fallen in sympathy with the rest of the stock market: but in my view, it has become a relatively much better bargain, especially with strong production expected in FY25 and deliveries ahead of expectations so far in Q1. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy rating on this company.

Stronger-than-expected deliveries presents a contrast to Tesla

To me, one of the core reasons that Rivian is such an appealing buy now is that its deliveries have been reasonably strong, while Tesla has fallen victim to global protests over its brand - not just in the U.S., but across the Atlantic in Europe as well, especially in Germany where Musk has had very public feuds with the German government.

The chart above showcases Tesla's production and deliveries for Q1. The company's 336.7k delivery units, reported in early April, fell roughly -11% below consensus estimates for 377k deliveries, while also declining -13% y/y versus 387k deliveries in the year-ago quarter. This is an indication of the growing backlash against Elon Musk that is cementing itself in today's zeitgeist, and is a troubling look for the world's largest EV maker ahead of its planned launch of the next-gen, lower-cost Model 3 and Model Y variants.

The situation for Tesla is so dim that Elon Musk held a widely-publicized all hands meeting with Tesla employees in an attempt to convince them to stay with the company and hold on to their shares, talking up future innovations such as the Optimus robot. But no matter how much spin is put on the situation, there is little denial that Tesla's sales momentum has slowed dramatically.

Rivian isn't exactly growing like a weed, especially after a planned factory shutdown this year (to convert lines ahead of its planned R2 model launch) and a shortage of a critical component. But even though Rivian's deliveries are in a y/y decline (which is to be expected ahead of a key new product launch, as buyers wait for the new model to come out), results are better than expected. Also in April on the exact same day that Tesla reported its Q1 production and delivery metrics, Rivian reported 8,640 cars delivered - which came in 8% higher than the "approximately 8,000" vehicles that the company had originally guided to. Rivian also confirmed its FY25 delivery target of 46-51k vehicles, the high end of which would represent roughly flat y/y performance versus 51.5k vehicles delivered in FY24 - again, not a bad result ahead of the R2 launch in FY26.

Relative to other companies, an escalating trade war may have less impact on Rivian

First off, no company in the world is immune to escalating trade wars. Some companies (like retailers that manufacture in China) will have direct, first-order impacts, while other companies will feel only a second-order sting from a slowing macroeconomy.

Rivian, in my view, falls somewhere in the middle of that spectrum: but relative to many other companies, Rivian has the potential to fare better amid escalating tariffs. For one, Rivian manufactures its vehicles primarily in the U.S. Importantly, U.S. manufacturing does not mean that Rivian's business is immune from tariffs, as the company surely sources at least some of its parts internationally (the company notes that its FY25 outlook incorporates tariff risks known at the time, but does not break out specific impacts).

However, given that one of the focuses of Trump's tariff plan is to reinvigorate U.S. manufacturing, I think that Rivian's $6.6 billion Biden-era loan from the Department of Energy, which was finalized in early January and will be disbursed over a multi-year period, is likely safe from being rescinded or downsized by the new administration - especially since the proceeds of the loan are intended to boost Rivian's R2 manufacturing capacity in Illinois. The company notes that the deal will help bring in 7,500 new manufacturing jobs.

Furthermore, Rivian is (at least for now) also relatively shielded from the impacts of retaliatory tariffs from a top-line perspective. Rivian does not break out its exact sales by region, but currently the company sells only into the U.S. with a minor portion of sales in Canada.

Tesla, however, has massive international exposure. The U.S. was just shy of half of the company's FY24 revenue, with China representing 21% of FY24 sales and the rest of the world (primarily Europe) representing another 30%.

China has already instituted retaliatory tariffs against the U.S. The European Commission, which has to coordinate among its member states to decide on a response, is preparing similar moves. In both cases, sharper import duties will contribute to an already-weak sales environment and negative brand backlash in these key markets.

Margin progress

Lastly, one of the most important long-term indicators that has me enthusiastic on Rivian is its continued progress in gross margins. In Q4, Rivian hit a 10% gross margin, scoring above breakeven for the first time in the company's history, as manufacturing scales have matured, and the company enjoys a proportionally larger mix of revenue from software and services.

Management notes that it has removed an incredible $31,000 of COGS out of each vehicle unit sold in Q4, relative to the prior-year Q4. The company also notes that it is 95% complete with finalizing the bill of materials for the upcoming R2 (with mass production slated to begin in mid-2026), with a BOM that is roughly half of the current R1 models (giving the company plenty of room to slash prices relative to the current R1S and R1T models and finally push Rivian into the mass market). Per CEO and founder RJ Scaringe's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Now, of course, the things we see in terms of development of our product and the build out of the expansion to our plant, those are very visible to someone driving by a normal or to see our test vehicles out on the road. But we've also sourced 95% of the bill of materials. And the bill of materials in R2 is expected to be roughly half of what we have in R1. And the other non-bill of material COGS targets are significantly less than what we have in R1, actually significantly less than half of what we have in R1. And so, the overall cost structure that we're planning for an R2 is a night and day improvement relative to what we launched with and what we have today on our R1."

Needless to say, we'll have to see how tariffs imposed on internationally sourced parts will play a role into Rivian's BOM development. Still, we note the continuous improvement in the company's cost profile and the heavy cost takeouts for next year's model give Rivian a great baseline to work with.

Key takeaways

As always, in hyper-volatile situations, investors should avoid giving into the surrounding negative emotions and focus on long-term drivers. Rivian, in my view, is a perfect buy-the-dip candidate because it may benefit from tariffs charged on foreign automakers (plus cultural backlash against Tesla), while its primarily domestic sales base shields it from foreign retaliation. At the same time, the company continues to make progress toward achieving richer gross margins, while marching ahead with its lower-cost R2 models that are expected to hit the road in 2026.

Stay long here and use the drop as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Microsoft: I Was Wrong (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ:MSFT) on November 20th, 2024 with my article “Microsoft: Basing Before Next Moves Higher (Rating Upgrade)”, the stock was attempting to recover from prior selling pressure that ultimately resulted in a period of prolonged weakness that extended throughout most of the second-half of the year. In the article, my bullish argument centered around strong evidence of earnings and revenue growth during the previous quarterly period with relative stability in the stock’s forward valuation metrics. Following publication, the stock did manage to rally to highs of $456.17 in a little over three weeks (indicating gains of 9.43%). However, those gains were short-lived, and the stock quickly reversed at rates that were quick enough to encounter losses of -19.49% by the end of March 2025. During this process, MSFT fell below my $385 pivot point, and this trend erosion has forced me to revise my outlook in a more bearish manner. As a result, I will be lowering my stand to a “hold” rating until I see better evidence that the stock has formed a truly sustainable base.

First, I will start with what I found to be the most positive aspects in Microsoft’s fiscal second-quarter 2025 earnings figures. Revenue and EPS figures beat consensus expectations by a comfortable margin (at $69.63 billion and $3.23 per share, respectively). Microsoft’s A.I. segment is also showing strong growth in revenue, and the business has now reached yearly sales of $13 billion. Revenue growth for the period indicated an annualized growth rate of 12.3%, but you would have to go back to 2023 to find weaker rates of growth. Annualized gains in Microsoft’s net income figure of $24.11 billion were even lower (at 10.24%). One area where Microsoft failed to match consensus estimates was in the Intelligent Cloud segment, which did manage to generate $25.54 billion in revenue and growth rates of 19% for the period. The Azure subset fared a bit better, with annualized gains of 31%, but this is still slower than the 33% growth rate that was posted during the fiscal first-quarter period. Revenue guidance for the third-quarter period was also disappointing, with expectations now seen posting within a range of just $67.7-68.7 billion.

Thus far, the market’s reaction to this performance hasn’t been much of a surprise, and we have seen several important support levels break during the stock’s recent decline. Unfortunately, these moves in share prices have done little to change Microsoft’s comparative position when assessed in relation to the rest of the MAG-7 stocks. Even at these lower levels, Microsoft is still trading with a forward price-earnings ratio of 28.3x, which is more expensive than most of the mega-cap cohort. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) at 28.15x and Apple Inc. (AAPL) at 27.79x are trading within relatively close proximity to Microsoft, but there are still several stock opportunities that are trading at much more attractive valuations. For example, it can be argued that Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) offer much better buying opportunities (with forward price-earnings metrics of 21.02x and 17.02x, respectively). Even Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is trading at cheaper levels right now (at 22.5x) and this might be surprising for many investors because this is a stock that is often criticized as being overvalued. At the moment, only Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is trading with less attractive forward price-earnings metrics, and this is one factor that should take some of the wind out of the sails for anyone who is considering entering into new MSFT long positions.

In large part, the price charts themselves align with these fundamental factors because several important support zones have been breached in these recent moves to the downside. One factor that I will mention here is the series of higher highs that began with the August 5th, 2024 lows. Of course, this was a very bearish and very memorable day for tech stocks, and the resulting uptrend in share price momentum that materialized after this session has now broken in the downward direction. I would never place a trade based on trend lines alone, but what this tells us is that something in the paradigm has shifted. Regions of trend support that were working in the past are no longer viable, and this does tell us (as long investors) that we might need to make some reassessments. I also want to point out the fact that a lot of this weakness was signaled in advance because there was a bearish divergence in the relative strength index (first arrow, to the left), and then the prevailing trend momentum reading was bearish with this indicator during the months that followed.

We will move on to some more advanced forms of analysis next and look at the stock’s recent standard deviation price channels in connection with the recent formation of reversal patterns. MSFT recently fell into its -2 standard deviation price channel, and this is significant because only 2.5% of all price action would be expected to be contained within this territory. In addition to this, there is a bullish harmonic structure (NenStar) that has formed in this timeframe. To clarify, a NenStar harmonic pattern is not as “cosmic” as it sounds. In reality, this price pattern is based on a series of Fibonacci retracements and a bit of a unique structural shape. What is important to understand here is that we have seen a fairly excessive price move to the downside, and it comes in conjunction with the fact that harmonic structures are also forming in the same area.

On the weekly timeframe, MSFT share prices have clearly broken below the -2 standard deviation. Of course, this is more significant in this timeframe and this is why I have not completely abandoned my position. In some ways, this type of event could be considered a “buy” signal, but my current Elliott Wave count is at odds with this outlook. In the chart below, we can see that there is a potential 1.618 C-wave extension, which implies that share prices might fall below $323, and indicator readings in the moving average convergence divergence (MACD) are oversold on this timeframe. On balance, the weekly chart is essentially giving us one piece of bearish evidence and two pieces of bullish evidence. For these reasons, I will still be holding part of my long position rather than giving up completely on the potential for a rebound.

Overall, I think that we can easily identify multiple areas of weakness in Microsoft’s most recent earnings report, and it is difficult to argue with the fact that the stock’s forward valuation metrics are not exceedingly attractive when compared to the rest of the MAG-7 cohort. Market volatility over the last few weeks has done a lot to invalidate my prior bullish stance because several price levels that would normally function as regions of support failed to live up to my expectations. As a result, I will be downgrading my position to a “hold” rating because I think it is possible that the stock might continue to fall further before we start to see meaningful rallies once again. An upside break of $396.34 (highs from March 25th, 2025) would invalidate this more bearish outlook and if we see this upside break occur, I will revise my current outlook.

data.title: We Have 30 Days To Fix This
data.description:
Yesterday’s closing values for the major market indexes made it appear we finally had a day of relative calm, but the volatility was one for the record books. The S&P 500 swung from a loss of nearly 5% at the open to a gain of 3.4% within a half hour when a rumor surfaced that President Trump would be delaying the implementation of tariffs for 90 days to have time for negotiations. The trading range for the Russell 2000 small-cap index was more than 10%. I have never seen such extreme volatility. We don’t know the source of the rumor or the reason it was spread, but it clearly indicates that when we do transition from tariffs to trade deals, the upside in this market is tremendous. I think we have 30 days to make this happen before tariffs run the risk of ending this economic expansion.

The reason is that the stock market has rapidly priced in the recession most think would occur if the tariffs proposed are enacted this week and remain in place. While the hard economic data still suggests we have a healthy economy, as indicated by last week’s jobs report, the negative wealth effect that will result from a prolonged downturn in financial assets is likely to result in a negative feedback loop. The difference between this year’s selloff and prior market swoons that resulted in recessions is that this one is self-inflicted. A change in trade policy, even a moderation, would likely lead to a rapid recovery in risk asset prices, as we briefly saw yesterday.

The Trump administration has dismissed concerns about the market’s collapse, indicating that the drop in energy prices and long-term interest rates that have accompanied it will benefit consumers. That is true. The problem is that the decline in both is connected to fears the economy will be in recession this year, which would wipe out the benefits of both for the bottom half of American households that don’t own stocks. The top 10% own most of the stock market wealth, which is why they account for 50% of our consumer spending. If this stock market decline lasts much longer than the month of April with no meaningful recovery, then the top 10% will undoubtedly cut back on spending, which is the lifeblood of our economic growth.

When consumer spending starts to decline for more than a month or two, it results in job losses. Those job losses lead to the loss of even more spending power, resulting in a negative feedback loop, as less spending leads to more job losses, which leads to even less spending. At the same time, the inflation that tariffs would cause in the months ahead, even if just a one-time price increase, would wipe out the real (inflation-adjusted) wage gains for those who don’t have any stock market wealth. This is why a stock market recovery over the coming month is critical to avoiding a recession in 2025. The good news is that all it would take is a transition from punitive tariffs to trade deals.

This is why many supporters of the administration are now breaking ranks, including Republicans members of Congress, and financial titans like Bill Ackman, Ken Griffin, Stan Druckenmiller, and even Elon Musk, who has called for a “free-trade zone” between the US and EU. They see the damage coming down the road from trade policy as it stands now, which is being reflected in market prices today. They are encouraging negotiations over more retaliation, as we saw yesterday in the president’s threat to increase the tariff on Chinese imports to more than 100%.

Again, I am still convinced that we will see announcements that deals are replacing tariffs, as the pressure from Main Street, Wall Street, and members of the president’s own party build in intensity. It needs to happen before the negative wealth effect takes hold of our economy. I think we have 30 days to fix this.

Over the weekend, I indicated that a 50% retracement for the bull market in the S&P 500 to 4816 was a likely support level where we would find buyers. Yesterday’s low was 4835. At that point, stocks bounced and were supercharged with the rumor that swept across trading desks an hour later. We are seeing more follow through this morning, which is surprising given the president’s new threat that he will increase the tariff rate on Chinese imports by another 50%. I don’t expect today’s rally to last long, as any bounce in stocks may only serve to embolden the president in his aggressiveness with our trading partners. That said, the 4800 level, or not far beneath it, may serve as a durable bottom if tensions start to ease. Bottoms are processes and not events, so we need to be prepared for huge amounts of volatility over the coming weeks as we move closer to a resolution of this trade conflict. The current level of tariffs are not sustainable for long.

data.title: It Is Candy Store Time For Income Investors, This Is How And What To Buy
data.description:
The last couple of days have been tough to say the least. The investors that have been hit the hardest are exactly those who decided to take more risk and buy the promise of significant AI-powered growth.

However, it would be wrong to say that value investors have managed to avoid the plunge. In fact, if we look at the major asset classes, we will see that the correlations have converged to "1". Namely, there is a difference among the various asset classes and their level of decline, but what is common among them all (or most of them) is they have registered relatively meaningful losses. Be it private credit (BIZD), midstream (AMLP), real estate (VNQ) or high-yield (USHY) - none of them have avoided the pain.

If we look at the dividends stocks, then we will see a very similar picture, which gives a fully legitimate ground for passive income investors to become concerned about their current income streams.

I will not lie, these current market dynamics consume a lot of my thoughts.

Here is a list of various aspects, risks and scenarios I have been thinking about lately and contextualizing with my portfolio/capital allocation strategy:

  • The "Fed put" is likely out of the money due to inflationary effects from the introduction of massive tariffs across the board. This is a notable exception to what we have been used to since the GFC.
  • The "Trump put" is highly unpredictable, so that basing any investment decisions on that would be too premature and speculative (e.g., we still do not know anything about the tax cuts).
  • The notion of tariffs serving as a bargaining tool to strike better trade deals is only partially correct. While I agree that sooner or later, we will see some better outcomes from the trade negotiations, it would be too optimistic to assume that the U.S. will succeed with all of the countries. The tariffs are imposed in order to solve the trade deficits. It is hard to imagine how major economies could offer the President a solution that would suddenly neutralize the trade deficits that have existed for many decades. And it is hard to imagine how Trump could agree on a deal that does not deliver on this.
  • There are both direct and indirect consequences to the businesses, which together bode ill for future growth prospects. For example, it is not only about the tariffs, higher costs and margin compression, but it is also about the overall slowdown in the CapEx plans, consumers holding back their spending, foreign investments being paused due to, among other things, a weakening USD etc.

In a nutshell, we have real economic reasons to assume major headwinds to the U.S. businesses and the asset prices accordingly.

Having said that, the reason why I have been thinking about all of this is not driven by fear (although, I could see that easily happening if I was Mag-7 fan). I will elaborate on this in the next section.

Instead, I feel like my 6-year old son, who is in the candy store with unlimited interest, but a limited budget to buy.

The real question is whether to load up the truck now at the current discounts, or do it gradually, assuming that the next candy store that is some blocks away from this one will have better deals.

My approach is to simply follow a dollar-cost-averaging approach by reinvesting all of the dividends and committing part of my salary each month into high-quality and durable income assets.

However, it is equally important to understand what to buy, which is a nice segue to the next section.

My strategy as a durable income investor

The only reason why I do not panic and can sleep well at night is purely related to my investment philosophy. As I have laid out it in many of my previous articles, I am a passive income investor with an objective to achieve a certain amount of current income streams in a certain period of time.

Under such a construct, the stock prices are really irrelevant. In fact, I would even argue that the lower stock prices we get, the better it is for hitting the necessary dividend income target - i.e., lower prices mean higher yields.

Yet, things are, obviously, not that straightforward. We still have to appreciate the risks such as those that I outlined above. Hence, it is very critical to properly calibrate new investments in order to accelerate the process of building the passive income snowball, while making sure that we can still sleep well at night and that the dividends will not get cut.

In essence, as long as we are exposed to truly sustainable dividends and have the income-focused objective in place, then we can easily start to capitalize on the current dislocations.

And as indicated earlier, since the correlation among most of the asset classes has been strong, it inevitably creates pockets of opportunity for investors to deploy capital into those securities that have been punished too hard (or together with the high-tech and excessive valuation stocks).

So, these are the factors I focus on when investing for durable income now:

  • Excellent track-record in terms of distributing consistent dividends across many different cycles, such as the GFC and COVID-19.
  • Robust capital structures with, preferably, investment grade or even upper investment grade balance sheets.
  • High dividend coverage level that leaves equity capital for growth and shock-absorption.
  • Defensive business models that are not dependent on consumer discretionary spending or demand that is there only when the economy goes up.
  • No or very minimal exposure to export and import markets.

By considering the above, we automatically rule out many growth-tilted areas and, certainly, speculative bets or companies that have not yet started paying out sustainable dividends.

The sectors that tend to have companies that embody the aforementioned factors are not many. In my view, I see infrastructure, energy and REITs as areas, where the passive income investments could be directed.

Granted, there are certainly exceptions to be found in currently above-average risk departments as well, such as private credit, tech and global blue-chips, but not at the same scale as in the sectors that I just mentioned.

For example, AMLP, which is an index that tracks midstream securities is down by almost 10% since the President embarked on new tariffs. Many high-quality midstream gems have suffered accordingly even though their fundamentals are stronger than ever.

One of my current high-conviction picks in this space is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which has paid out solid dividends without any cuts for 26 consecutive years. It has survived much more severe shocks like the GFC and COVID-19. We can for sure make a case that the production volumes of gas and oil will shrink, and thus EPD's cash flows could get negatively affected. Yet, there is also a basis for making the counterargument that given the organic CapEx projects that will come online this year in combination with cheaper energy prices driving the consumption, we could still see a nice positive cash flow growth from EPD.

But more importantly, EPD has an upper investment grade balance sheet with a distribution coverage of 170%. In addition, the majority of the cash flows are based on strong long-term agreements that mitigate both the volumetric and price risks. Also, EPD has not ventured into massive CapEx projects (below 50% of the annual EBITDA generation), which gives great flexibility to management on making either accretive M&A or opportunistic share buybacks.

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) is another infrastructure-related darling I like. It has declined by ~5% in the past couple of days. The dividend yield has now reached almost 8%. The beauty of UTG is that offers a diversified exposure to different infrastructure types and companies. The lion's share of its Top 10 holdings are investment grade and operate in inherently durable sectors such as telco towers, power utilities and gas distribution. These are not export-oriented businesses and, generally, enjoy non-cyclical demand. Also, in most cases the cash flows are linked to long-term/regulated contracts. Since the GFC UTF has not cut its dividend, which includes the COVID-19 period (i.e., a much tougher test for the infrastructure businesses that we see now).

Another area that I like is high-quality equity REITs, and, particularly, REITs with long-term lease contracts. Typically, these are net lease, industrial, telecommunication and data center REITs (office as well, but I do not think that it would be wise to seek durable dividend there now). It is important to have the long-term contracted cash flow component in place as it helps de-risk potential pressures on pricing and new vacancy rates.

Realty Income Corporation (O) is a great example that I am currently buying. It has an almost 10-year weighted average lease maturity schedule and in-place rents that have been signed at below market-level rates, which will come in handy when negotiating new pricing terms with either the existing or new tenants. Moreover, O has one of the best balance sheets in the REIT space - i.e., an upper investment grade credit rating. The FFO payout of 75% leaves ample space for management to act opportunistically or just sit on the sidelines, executing the current buyback program. O is also one of the few REITs out there that has a dividend aristocrat status (i.e., paying dividends for more than 25 years in a row). The only theoretical issue with O is that the majority of its tenants are non-investment grade. Here we have to keep in mind that the lion's share of O's tenant roster operates in segments that are inherently non-cyclical and that the portfolio itself is extremely well-diversified. For instance, in the COVID-19 days, O did not only manage to avoid falling into the unsustainable dividend coverage territory (FFO going below dividends), but it also signed sizable M&A deals in a very accretive manner.

The bottom line

In my view, growth and high-tech investors are in deep trouble. If I was in those shoes, I would seriously consider pulling out capital from the portfolio and exploring areas to take shelter.

Luckily, as a pure-play dividend investor I do not have to change the investment course. Or let me put it another way, as long as the objective is tied to current income streams and not selling stocks to fund the planned consumption, then we have a completely different ball game than many investors, who are currently panicking.

I would call it a "dividend put", which gets into the money, when stock prices fall, but the thing that matters most - dividend income - remains protected.

If we can really switch off the emotions and stick to the original game plan of living off the passive income, then the next step is to do some buying.

In this article, I have outlined my approach and selected picks for capitalizing on the current opportunities in the market.

data.title: 'Unexpectedly' Strong March 2025 Jobs Report Sees Gains For Teens
data.description:
The March 2025 employment situation report came in stronger than expected. The report, which had been expected to show 140,000 non-farm jobs added to the U.S. economy, instead reported an increase of 228,000 jobs to 159,398,000.

The portion of the jobs report, based on the BLS' monthly household survey, likewise saw increases. The non-seasonally adjusted number of Age 16 or older individuals counted as having jobs increased by 868,000. Of this number, 142,000 (16%, or a little more than 1 out of 6) were teenagers. More remarkably, 114,000 (13%, or a little more than 1 out of 8) were younger teens Age 16 and 17.

That increase is remarkable is because younger teens make up only 1.1% of the 163,412,000 Americans estimated to be employed in the non-seasonally adjusted measure. Because this data is collected through a survey that contacts a sample of U.S. households, there are questions of how accurate the survey is when collecting employment data about this relatively small portion of the U.S. labor force.

After adjusting for seasonality, which smooths out recurring annual variations in the jobs data, the household survey counts a total of 163,508,000 employed people in the U.S. during March 2025. That figure represents an increase of 201,000 from the previous month.

Overall, the seasonally adjusted number of teens Age 16-19 dipped slightly from February 2025's total to 5,786,000. However, the seasonally adjusted number of younger teens Age 16-17 increased by 101,000 to 2,095,000, while the seasonally adjusted number of older teens Age 18-19 declined by 93,000 to 3,708,000.

We track changes in teen employment because these changes can have an outsize impact on the overall jobs numbers. The following chart presents seasonally adjusted U.S. teen employment and the teen employed-to-population ratio from January 2021 through March 2025.

You'll notice the seasonally adjusted numbers don't quite add up, which is because the employment data for each reported demographic gets its own seasonal adjustment. The non-seasonally adjusted data does add up, however, which is why we led with those numbers in this analysis.

References

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Labor Force Statistics (Current Population Survey - CPS). [Online Database]. Accessed: 4 April 2025.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bloom Energy: 2024 Year-End Record-Breaking Results Fail To 'Wow' Investors And Reduce Volatility
data.description:
Investment Thesis

We previously rated Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) as a speculative hold for risk neutral investors with a long position in clean energy stocks. When we initiated coverage, the stock was trading at approximately $24, and we were of the opinion that much of the upside potential coming from key projects announced in Q4-24 (i.e. the 1-GW agreement with AEP and the 80-MW single-site installation in South Korea) had already been accounted for into its share price.

Our revised DCF valuation of $19.08 per share represents a 10.5% downward adjustment from our previous target: $21.32. This revised valuation reflects and reinforces our belief that the margin for near-term appreciation, compared to post-earnings' highs, is limited.

Given the current share price ranging between $16 and $18, we are revising our previous rating, upgrading it to a speculative buy for risk neutral, long-term investors.

Following the year-ended 2024 earnings release, which met expectations, we continue to believe that, whilst BE has a solid case for growth in the long term, the margin for near-term appreciation over recent highs is limited. We also acknowledge recent developments in the AI space and, more broadly, the wider economy. In late January 2025, technology and AI-related stocks, including BE, were severely impacted by DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), a Chinese startup, that released a low-cost AI model with the alleged ability to disrupt the U.S. AI and Data Center industries. More recently, on April 4th, China responded to the U.S.'s 10% trade tariffs on imports by announcing an additional 34% tariff on U.S. goods. On both occasions, BE experienced a significant drop in share price: from a $29.83 high to $20.40 in late January 2025, and from $19.45 to $16.60 between April 3rd and 4th.

We are of the view that it is too soon to cast a judgment on how the data center industry will be affected by DeepSeek and on the long-term impact of the China-US "trade war". We note that the stock is still highly volatile, rendering it heavily susceptible to external news (BE was amongst Friday's top losers) and upcoming developments in recent tariff announcements are likely to further impact BE's share price. Whilst taking into account current developments, however, we remain of the opinion that BE has a long-term capability to cater for the growing energy needs of AI-driven data centers and utilities. Based on these considerations, we are of the view that the stock is now trading slightly under its current value.

We see the recent drop in Bloom Energy's share price to the $16-18 range, currently lower than our estimated fair value of $19.08, as an opportunity to selectively re-enter or add to existing positions rather than de-risk. The decline is not connected with BE's fundamentals, but rather to broader market news weighing on AI-related stocks and wider macroeconomic updates. Whilst we can expect persistent weakness in the coming weeks amid uncertainty in the market, we believe the current range is already suitable to commence re-entering or accumulating their position. We see an opportunity to cautiously accumulate at current levels, particularly for risk neutral, long-term investors. Risk-averse investors should hold until an improvement in general sentiment or a price floor is identified, even if this may mean missing out on earlier gains. For those who previously considered trimming their position, be it investors who entered between 13th April 2023 and 14th November 2024 (i.e. a 20-month period during which BE never traded above $19.00) or, more broadly, investors who were able to capture significant gains, a phased re-entry strategy is also recommended. A phased re-entry strategy would help manage the short-term downside risk.

Valuation Analysis

We have revised our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model of Bloom Energy by including 2024's year-end figures in the historical data component used to form our growth assumptions.

Based on the most recent financial information and revised assumptions, the DCF model estimates Bloom Energy's fair value to be $19.08 (down from $21.32) per share. This re-affirms our view that the share was previously overvalued (i.e. between November 2024 and March 2025). Hence, we remain of the opinion that the previous highs had locked in the upside potential of the company's catalysts without fully accounting for its associated risks.

Our DCF takes into account strategic projects and partnerships such as the AEP agreement, the South Korea installation project, and the recently announced partnership with Chart Industries. Furthermore, the DCF model is based on the application of historical information taken from the company's 10-k reports, and broader assumptions on expected growth and risks.

Historical Information Available - A 10-year data set was reviewed and utilized to develop base expectations on growth. Furthermore, although 'product accepted' metric is no longer included in the company's financials, product acceptance volumes for 2022, 2023 and TTM (Q3-24) were used to form an assumption basis for Product Revenue growth in the years 2025–2030.

Upcoming Project Assumptions - Assumptions for the AEP agreement and the South Korea installation project were maintained in line with the previous DCF, as no substantial changes pertaining to these two catalysts occurred since the previous valuation. In addition to the existing projects' assumptions, a new revenue line was added for carbon capture solutions expected to be developed from the partnership with Chart Industries. By offering this new solution, BE reinforces its position in the green economy, further catering to those in need of carbon efficient solutions. Our base case assumes that a pilot project may be launched this year, whilst wider-scale adoption would gradually commence from 2026.

Growth Projections - The model considers year-on-year revenue growth, which we are expecting to be particularly significant over the next two years due to the materialization of key projects such as the AEP Agreement, the South Korea Installation Project, and the Carbon Capture Partnership with Chart Industries.

EBIT margins are also expected to gradually improve over the next five years. This is based on the assumption that costs per-unit will be reduced through economies of scale, and operational optimizations will create cost efficiencies.

Financial Performance

Revenue - As at year-end, Bloom Energy reported a year-on-year 10.5% growth in annual revenue: $1,473.8 million compared to $1,333.5 million in 2023. Bloom Energy was able to be within its 2024 revenue guidance thanks to the 73.2% quarter-on-quarter revenue growth witnessed in Q4-24, when revenue grew to $572.4 million.

Concentration risk remains high, with 53% of revenue being generated from three customers, one of which is a related party and accounts for 23% of total revenue.

The company's core revenue stream, Products, increased by 11.3% year-on-year, from $975.2 million to $1,085.2 million. Similarly, its Servicing and Installation streams witnessed an increase: 16.6% (from $183.1 million to $213.5 million) and 31.8% (from $92.8 million to $122.3 million), respectively. The Electricity revenue stream was the only one that experienced a decline, from $82.4 million to $52.8 million: 35.8%.

Compared to prior years, the share of internationally generated revenue declined, from 44% in 2022, to 30% in 2023, to 26% in 2024.

Gross Margins - Bloom Energy reported a 27.5% gross margin, with $1,069.2 million in cost of revenue, leading to a gross profit of $404.6 million. Compared to 2023, when a $197.8 million gross profits was recorded, cost of revenue declined 5.9% from $1,135.7 million. It must, however, be noted that non-GAAP adjustments, which were particularly high in 2023 due to a $123.7m impairment of assets relating to the decommissioning of server units upon repowering bring the 2023 (non-GAAP) gross profit up to $344.0 million and its gross margins to 25.8%. The non-GAAP figures give us confidence that there is a gradual improvement in the overall gross margins.

Balance Sheet - Bloom Energy's debt-to-equity ratio remains high at 3.54. Total liabilities as of the end of 2024 amount to $2,072.1 million, 9.5% higher than the $1,893.0 million total liabilities as of 2023's year-end. The rise in liabilities was primarily driven by higher recourse debt, which increased from $842.0 million in 2023 to $1,124.7 million in 2024. Of this, $114.4 million is due within the next 12 months. Additionally, Deferred Revenue and Customer Deposits surged by 93.4% from $148.1 million to $286.4 million, indicating an increase in customer commitments.

Despite the increase in liabilities, Bloom Energy's quick ratio remains stable at around 2.4, indicating a strong liquidity position. Accounts Receivable amount to $335.8 million, virtually unchanged from 2023's $340.7 million. However, 76% of this balance is attributable to three key customers, one of which is a related party that, alone, makes up for 28% of total receivables. Whilst the related party portion reduced significantly compared to 2023, when it amounted to 76% of the total receivables, concentration risk remains high given the dependency on these three key customers.

Catalysts

Scaling AI-Driven Power Demand - We are of the opinion that whilst the upside potential emanating from the projects announced in Q4-24 has already been factored in the share price, the successful outcome of these projects could pave the way towards consolidating Bloom Energy's position as a leading provider of sustainable power solutions for the AI sector. As such, potential trigger points that could positively impact BE's share price include announcements similar to that of the AEP procurement agreement made back in November 2024. At the latest earnings call, KR Sridhar, Bloom Energy's Founder/Chairman/CEO, did mention that the company is currently in talks with "several utilities who are interested in some kind of arrangements along the lines of what was announced with AEP" and whilst he appeared confident that such types of deals is bound to materialize, he did not provide a timeframe. The adoption and utilization of AI is expected to surge data centers' power demands; a recent study by Goldman Sachs Research suggests that power demand could increase by 165% by 2030 causing increasing data centers' share of electricity consumption from 1-2% to 2-4% globally. If AI-driven power demand proceeds to scale in this manner, it will be of paramount importance for Bloom Energy to be able to meet its large-scale deployment projects across data centers: supply chain resilience and manufacturing capacity will be key in sustaining this. However, we must also continue to monitor developments emerging from DeepSeek's recent announcement of a cost-effective solution which could have a negative impact on the U.S. AI industry.

Chart Industries Carbon Capture partnership - Bloom Energy and Chart Industries are partnering to integrate their solid oxide fuel cell technology and carbon capture solutions. According to Bloom Energy, the partnership "Will pave the way for a cost-effective path to decarbonization using natural gas and sequestration". This, in turn, could create a scalable and sustainable approach to meet the growing energy needs of data centers and other energy-intensive consumers through the use of Fuel Infrastructure. If successful, this partnership would render fuel cell technology an attractive solution for customers under ESG and Regulatory pressures, potentially placing Bloom Energy in a position to capitalize on the growing need for sustainable energy solutions.

Risks

Regulatory Uncertainty - Whilst existing policies may support clean energy, we do not expect similar policies to be developed in the near-future. For instance, the Investment Tax Credit (ITC) for fuel cells expired in 2024 and whilst some projects may still be eligible under the ITC bracket, we do not envision similar incentives to be developed in the US under the Trump administration.

Concentration Risk - With 53% of revenue being generated from three customers, one of which is a related party accounting for 23% of total revenue, concentration risk remains high customer level. Similarly, from a credit perspective, three customers, one of which is a related party, accounted for 74% of accounts receivable. The related party accounted for 28% of total receivables. Both from a revenue and credit perspective, the dependency on these three customers creates a high concentration risk. A reduction or loss in business from one of these customers, or a default in receivable payments, would have a material impact on the company's financial performance.

Financial Sustainability - Since inception, Bloom Energy has incurred in approximately $3.9 billion in losses. With a beta of 3.28, the stock's volatility renders it highly susceptible to broader market fluctuations. In adverse macroeconomic or risk-off conditions, this could lead the company to failure in meeting operational and financial targets, potentially causing extensive losses and negative cash flow. In turn, this would likely result in the need for additional financing, exposing the company subject to uncertainty in future interest rates and access to capital markets.

China-U.S. "trade war" - Recent tariff impositions by the U.S. and China have led to panic across the global stock markets on April 4th. Escalation of trade tensions between the U.S. and China have unlocked fears of an imminent recession: J.P. Morgan revised the chances of a global recession taking place before year-end from 40% to 60%. In the event of a recession or a significant downturn, Bloom Energy may experience short-term challenges such as potential customer project postponements, drop in new orders driven by an overall sense of reduced urgency for capital deployment, limited funding availability and higher borrowing costs. Additionally, increased adversity to risk by investors could negatively affect BE's share price.

Conclusion

Bloom Energy met 2024 expectations and has a promising outlook for 2025, with ongoing large-scale projects that are positioning the company to become a lead infrastructure provider in the AI space. Emerging macroeconomic factors are setting the Bloom Energy's market valuation aback and will need to be attentively monitored to ensure they do not hinder the company's overall financial sustainability (see "Risk" section above). We are of the opinion that whilst much of the upside potential coming from key projects announced in Q4-24 had already been accounted for in Bloom Energy's post-earnings share price, the current share price offers room for risk neutral, long-term investors who can brace through the ongoing concerns around a global recession, to buy-in in a phased manner.

data.title: With Consistent Misses And Falling Margins, KT Corp. Is No Bargain
data.description:
KT Corporation Overview

Back in September, I wrote up KT Corporation (NYSE:KT) and praised the South Korean telecom’s low price/book ratio and relatively strong earnings. At the same time, I warned the price was getting on the high side without meaningful growth and downgraded it to a hold. The rise continued, defying my expectations.

So here we are, as I’m writing this it’s Monday, which is being called “Black Monday” in the South Korean press. Clearly, times have changed, but KT is still riding high, and I think it’s worth examining where we stand in what’s shaping up to be a very different economic environment. We’ll go through the Q4 numbers released back in February, and contrast them to the expectations, and of course, we’ll ask whether this telecom is really worth considering as a flight to safety pick, given how popular such stocks are in these uncertain times.

Balance Sheet Remains Modestly Strong

Last time, the balance sheet was a big focus in my thesis, so I would be remiss if I didn’t feature it prominently again. It’s still not bad, though it’s not quite as intriguing as it was in September.

Then

Now

Cash and Equivalents

$2.8 billion

$2.5 billion

Total Current Assets

$11.3 billion

$9.7 billion

Total Assets

$32.0 billion

$28.4 billion

Total Current Liabilities

$10.5 billion

$9.4 billion

Total Liabilities

$18.0 billion

$16.2 billion

Total Shareholder Equity

$14.2 billion

$12.3 billion

Price/Book Ratio

0.53

0.68

(source: most recent financial 6-K from SEC)

Again, there’s nothing to be worried about here, though the won went from its 52-week high against the dollar in September down to its lowest point in over a decade. As the stock price goes up, the price/book ratio is also no longer as appealing as it once was at 0.68, though it’s still substantially better than the sector median of 1.82. There’s little to say about this in isolation from other details, suffice it to say KT still has a respectable cash position and should be ready to handle any near-term needs.

KT’s Earnings Miss Problem

KT capped off the year with a moderately disappointing Q4 with a loss. Now we don’t have quarterly earnings estimates with KT, so we can’t say it was a miss or not on a quarterly basis, annually however things aren’t looking great.

2023

2024

Operating Revenue

$17.9 billion

$18.0 billion

Operating Income

$1.1 billion

$550 million

EBITDA Margin

20.7%

17.7%

Net Income

$670 million

$310 million

EPADS

$1.51

65¢

(source: most recent financial 6-K from SEC)

KT reported record revenue, but once again this isn’t exactly a growth juggernaut, they just slightly exceeded the prior year. Everything further down the list looks substantially worse, with operating income being halved year over year, the EBITDA margin worsening, and earnings getting far, far worse, bringing them to a P/E ratio of 26.28.

That’s not exactly a value P/E range for KT, especially when one considers the sector median is 17.89. To put the problem in sharper focus, the 2024 earnings estimate was $3.48 per ADS, so this is a massive, massive miss. That KT has been flirting with 52-week highs after such a miss is nothing short of remarkable.

A huge, dramatic miss seems bad, and while the Q4 conference call tries to explain away the weakening operating income on one-time charges and workforce revamping, the issue goes deeper than that. KT has actually missed earnings estimates, often substantially, five straight years, and nine years out of the last ten.

So when I see the 2025 estimate of $13.19 billion in revenue and an EPADS of $3.01, I don’t doubt the revenue, but I feel like it’s prudent to have strong doubts about that earnings. KT’s estimates not only have a habit of being too high in that regard, but often wildly so. I wouldn’t care to hazard my own estimate of what they’ll earn, only that it’s almost certainly going to be less than that.

The Focus on AI Is Overstated

Like everybody and their brother, KT is emphasizing their effort on growing their cloud and AI business in a partnership with Microsoft (MSFT). On the one hand, it’s easy to understand why, because growth in KT’s primary business has stagnated while they enjoyed an 11.9% year-over-year revenue increase in their AI and IT business segment.

On the other hand, that’s still a minuscule segment in the big picture of KT. The revenue even after the growth puts them in the ballpark of KT Estate, which is the segment which deals with their office leases and hotel operations. Yet, we don’t mistake KT for a real estate focused company, nor should anyone view KT as an AI and IT company. They are a telecom company with some diverse side segments, but none of those are big enough to make a massive difference to the bottom line.

Competition and the Economy Are Meaningful Risks

So back in September I talked about how much KT continues to vie for market share with other substantial rivals in South Korea. That’s as true today as it was then, so I won’t revisit that but rather will focus on the new, massive issue that’s drawing a lot of attention, the global economy.

If you’ve followed the news over the last week, you know it’s not been great for the market in general, and as I mentioned before, I’m writing this on what South Korean media is calling “Black Monday.” Major tariffs and trade war prospects have many fearing a global recession, and South Korea is no different, scrambling to prop up their automakers in the face of the latest crisis.

KT isn’t an exporter, so they’re not directly impacted by the tariffs, but like literally everyone else on the planet, a weakened economy is bad for them. If there is indeed a global recession, as many expect to result from the tariffs, it would harm KT’s profitability and margins as well. They may have a strong enough balance sheet to weather the storm, but that doesn’t mean they’ll get through everything totally unscathed.

KT Stock Is Not That Appealing At These Levels

Having said all that about the dangerous economic situation, we should acknowledge that KT, like a lot of other telecoms, has done pretty well on the stock market of late as a flight to safety pick, with investors hoping it will be less impacted than others.

While that may be a case on the whole, less impacted is not the same as not impacted, and as the share price gets higher and higher, there is less and less reason to be impressed with what KT is offering for investors. I hate to downgrade a profitable company like this, but I’m going down to a slight sell, just slightly below a hold.

KT is not a terrible company by any means, but the consistent earnings misses and the weakening EBITDA margin are cause for concern. The dividend yield is 3.98%, which is roughly on par with the rest of the telecom sector’s 3.83%. Sustainability of the dividends may well become an issue at some point, as 2024’s dividends paid out were higher than their earnings.

There are better flights to safety out there, and I just don’t see the point in buying them at the high end of their 52-week range when their earnings performance suggests they oughtn’t be. This is just a bad time to buy, or probably even hold, KT.

Investors who do decide to ride this out hoping for a further flight to safety gain should keep a close eye on macroeconomic conditions, because if things get better and people aren’t desperate to stash their investments in something reliable, you could quickly see the safety premium dissolve and the price normalize to where it would otherwise be. In my view, that price would be lower, possibly quite a bit so if the earnings misses continue.

data.title: SDY: Aristocrats Can Beat In Uncertainty
data.description:
Fiscal 2025 turned out to be one of the most challenging years for stock market investors in the recent history as the trade war has triggered the risk of recession with slower growth in the following years. Furthermore, Wall Street also slashed corporation earnings forecasts as tariffs are likely to hit costs, prices and a supply chain. Consequently, the tech-heavy NASDAQ plunged into a bear market, while the S&P 500 is down 17% from its recent peak.

During tough conditions and increased uncertainty over the future, history shows that dividend investing has always helped investors beat the market and lower portfolio risk. In my opinion, SPDR® S&P Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SDY) is one of the best dividend-focused investment vehicles with a diversified portfolio of dividend aristocrats. The ETF appears well-positioned to outshine the broader market index and other risky strategies in the potential recession.

Stock Market Crash and Dividend Investing

The market fundamentals suggest that stocks can fall further, and the downtrend can last for a longer time because of increased uncertainty over how trade tariffs will impact the US economy and global trade.

The risk of recession increased significantly after the President Trump announced reciprocal tariffs on Liberation day. China responded with 34% tariffs on US products, while Canada and other countries are showing concerns over the US trade policy. Wall Street plummeted, with the NASDAQ falling into a bear market and the S&P 500 declined 17% from its recent peak.

Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs lifted the outlook of recession in 2025 to 60% and 45%, respectively. The Fed Atlanta expects the US GDP to come in around negative 2.8% amid a trade war. The two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth is technically described as a recession. The Fed Chairman stated that higher than expected tariffs are likely to bring inflation and slow economic growth prospects. Therefore, uncertainty over the rate cuts is likely to increase.

On the other hand, the Wall Street also began slashing earnings growth forecasts for 2025. The S&P 500 is now expected to deliver 7% earnings growth in the first quarter, compared to initial expectations for a double-digit increase. Overall, it appears that the challenging conditions are likely to continue in the second quarter. The trend can accelerate in the second half in the case of the US economy entering a recession. Historical trends also show that markets can fall significantly in recessionary periods.

Dividend investing is one of the best ways to deal with uncertainty. Unlike growth stocks, dividend stocks do not fall at a faster pace because of their stable business models and strong balance sheets. Most of the dividend-paying companies belong to non-cyclical sectors. These companies have low betas compared to the broader market index and growth stocks. Investors can further lower the risk factor by using a diversification factor. Exchange-Traded Funds are one of the best investment vehicles to gain exposure to a large number of dividend stocks through a single investment.

How Does SDY is a Solid Investment Option?

SPDR S&P Dividend ETF is one of the best investment options because of its portfolio of 150 dividend aristocrats. Companies with a history of 20 consecutive years of dividend growth are known as dividend aristocrats. These companies have established business models with the potential to generate steady revenue, earnings and cash flow growth over the long term.

SDY’s portfolio holdings are spread across all eleven S&P 500 sectors, with consumer defensive, utilities and industrials making up nearly 50% of the overall portfolio. These sectors are less correlated to economic cycles and market uncertainties. SDY’s top holdings from the consumer defensive sector include prominent names, such as Kimberly-Clark (KMB), Kenvue (KVUE), Coca-Cola (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), McDonald’s (MCD) and Sysco (SYY).

Whereas, companies in the utilities sector are benefiting from the robust growth in demand. Electrification and data centres are contributing to a growth in electricity demand in the US. Consequently, companies in the utilities sector announced record-breaking capital investment plans to improve their electricity generation potential. Consolidated Edison (ED), The Southern Co (SO), WEC Energy Group (WEC), Edison International (EIX) and Xcel Energy (XEL) are among its top holdings from the utilities sector.

Despite a market crash, Consolidated Edison continued to generate a solid share price upside. Its shares are up more than 20% year to date. The robust share price performance is attributed to its capital intensive plan and five-year adjusted earnings per share growth forecast of 6% to 7%. The Southern Company saw a share price growth of 8% year to date. Southern Company expects 6% earnings per share growth in 2025 with the plan of investing $63 billion in the next five years. WEC Energy also forecasts a long-term EPS growth rate of 6.5%-7% with a five-year investment plan of $28 billion. The company’s share price rallied 10% year to date.

From the industrials sector, SDY’s stake in the aerospace and defense companies are likely to deliver a solid earnings growth. The healthcare and financials stocks also account for a notable weight in its portfolio. The healthcare sector is likely to be one of the top contributors to the S&P 500’s earnings growth in 2025. SDY’s portfolio also offers exposure to dividend-paying companies from the technology, consumer cyclical, energy and basic materials.

The ETF’s earnings growth rate of nearly 8% appears strong enough to fuel share price and dividend returns. In addition, the ETF trades at attractive valuations. Its forward price to earnings ratio of 17.75 is lower than the S&P 500’s 20. Its trailing PE is also cheaper than the broader market index. Its dividend yield is around 2.8%, up from the S&P 500’s 1.45%. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.35% is lower than the median of all ETFs, while assets under management of $18 billion and average daily shares trading volume of 320K is enough for short and long-term investing. The fund is ranked 12th in its sub asset class with a quant score of 2.80.

Risk Factors to Consider

Although dividend stocks and ETFs are less risky than growth and tech stocks, they still carry a risk factor. There is no guarantee that investors' initial capital is completely safe. Despite a low beta, SDY shares also respond to broader stock price movements. Its stake in cyclical sectors, such as financials and real estate, can perform poorly in recessions and uncertain conditions. The fund's dividend growth rate also depends on the overall portfolio performance.

In Conclusion

Despite a risk factor, dividend investing is one of the great investment approaches to earn healthy risk-adjusted returns. In addition, investment vehicles like SDY can lower the risk factor compared to a single stock investment. The fund offers exposure to dividend-paying companies with the long histories and strong fundamentals. Its expense ratio is also low, valuations are cheap and liquidity is strong. Therefore, SDY could be a solid investment option to lower portfolio volatility in the potential recession.

data.title: OSI Systems: Backlog And Security Tailwinds Drive Momentum (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of OSI Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:OSIS) stock, it has gained nearly 22% in price.

OSI Systems has experienced strong momentum, driven primarily by robust growth in its Security division. With record Q2 FY25 results leading to upward revisions to guidance, the market has become rationally bullish on the stock recently. OSI's unprecedented $1.8 billion backlog reflects strong demand visibility and ongoing international expansion. When these wins are coupled with favorable geopolitical tailwinds due to OSI's ability to capitalize on growing global security infrastructure needs, the stock appears to be a clear Buy. However, beware of an elevated valuation with already high expectations from the market; the potential normalization of growth rates could temper investor enthusiasm in the medium term.

Operational & Fundamental Analysis

OSI operates across three divisions. The first of these is Security, which is the majority of its revenues, where it designs and manufactures security screening products and services globally. One of its key differentiators is its ability to provide turnkey security screening solutions, not just standalone scanners. Its Healthcare division is smaller, and operates under the Spacelabs Healthcare brand, where its monitors track vital signs for patients in critical and perioperative care. Finally, the company's Optoelectronics and Manufacturing division, which is larger than the Healthcare division but still substantially smaller than Security, gives the company a unique vertical integration. Through this division, the company designs and produces optoelectronic devices, printed circuit boards, and provides electronics manufacturing services ("EMS"); it serves both various end markets and its own Security and Healthcare products, and it differentiates itself through highly customizable solutions.

In January, the company delivered record Q2 FY25 earnings, and following that it raised full-year guidance. Key highlights include its record Security division backlog. This outlook ties in with broader company momentum and contract wins, particularly in its high-weight Security segment. FY25 guidance has actually increased twice so far, after Q1 and Q2. The positive revisions are fundamentally underpinned by OSI's execution. Management has demonstrated an ability to direct the company to convert its backlog to revenue efficiently. For example, it has ramped a major security contract in Mexico and been able to secure new deals to replenish that backlog.

In total, the backlog now sits at an unprecedented ~$1.8 billion in orders as of the last quarter; this is roughly equal to an entire year's revenue, providing a high degree of visibility for future sales. In Q2 FY25, OSI reported a book-to-bill ratio of 1.2. In particular, a hefty backlog in the Security division acts as a growth engine, and this should translate into double-digit revenue growth over the next 1-2 years as it is converted to sales.

Government agencies are currently investing heavily in security infrastructure. In the past year, OSI secured numerous contracts, including a $100 million award for cargo and vehicle inspection solutions in March 2024, a contract to supply advanced CT baggage scanners at a major European airport, and a $59 million order for cargo inspection systems in January 2024. It also won a 10-year security screening services contract in Uruguay and various mid-sized orders in the Middle East and Asia. Therefore, international expansion represents a substantial growth opportunity. New product launches like next-generation explosive trace detection ("ETD") systems also provide broader growth momentum. The company is benefiting from broader growth trends in the technology and security markets, a trend that is heightened given current geopolitical tensions and the outbreak of hot wars in the Middle East and Eastern Europe. In essence, these dynamics open up OSI stock as a momentum/growth play with secular tailwinds. OSI's security division grew by 27% year-over-year in Q4 FY24, closing out the last full financial year robustly.

While the Security division is particularly shining right now, OSI's Optoelectronics and Manufacturing division has also contributed positively, benefiting from increased manufacturing business. However, it may be subject to heavy tariffs as it has an international manufacturing presence, including in the United Kingdom, Mexico, India, Malaysia, and Indonesia. It also manufactures in the U.S., but expect margin pressures in the short to medium term as the company adapts to new trade barriers and further regionalizes production to fine-tune high margins from demand. The Healthcare division, while facing pressure due to financial challenges for hospitals, has shown signs of improvement, including securing a $4 million contract for patient monitoring solutions.

Price & Valuation Analysis (Including Risks)

Although OSI is benefiting from strong secular tailwinds, powerful product positioning, and elevating guidance, the stock price already reflects this fully. We can see evidence of this in the way the price has elevated above the 50-week moving average substantially. Only recently, during the broader market sell-off related to international trade war concerns, has OSI stock corrected significantly. However, this is not necessarily a buying opportunity; instead, it's a risk-on bet for alpha generation in a market with a heightened risk of recession right now.

There is a risk that market momentum is overweighting the company's medium-term growth prospects. A significant portion of OSI's growth has been driven by a catch-up in orders post-pandemic and a handful of very large contracts. The Mexico program in particular is past its peak, according to comments from the recent earnings calls, so if new orders don't match the size of these peak programs, OSI's organic growth could decelerate. There is a risk that 2024-2025 represents a high-water mark in growth that isn't maintained. In addition, government projects are inherently lumpy, and prone to delays. This reliance on government and defense spending also opens the company up to heightened policy risk, which could weigh on future growth and lead to a sentiment reversion, which is currently not indicated in forward guidance and current fundamentals. I think investors might be overlooking how cyclical and policy-dependent some of OSI's demand is, instead treating the backlog as if it will perpetually replenish itself.

Furthermore, as the company's normalized trailing P/E ratio is about 20, which is still higher than its five-year average of 18, the valuation assumes flawless execution. Forward diluted EPS growth is pegged at 18%, with 19.3% being the company's five-year trailing average. Therefore, this allocation requires a degree of caution. I am bullish, but reservedly so. An estimated $11 in TTM normalized EPS in April 2027 leads to a stock price of $220 if the forward P/E in one year is 20, slightly expanded due to major macro concerns likely abating by then. At the J.P. Morgan Industrials Conference on March 11, 2025, OSI Systems' Chief Financial Officer, Alan Edrick, addressed concerns regarding potential tariff impacts on the company. He stated that they will not significantly impact OSI, because the company has a diversified international production base to mitigate risks associated with region-specific tariffs by reallocating production as needed.

My $225 12-month price target for OSIS stock indicates a 28.5% upside from the present price of $175. However, due to the nature of the business (i.e., a mid-cap vulnerable to changes in policy, government contracts, and changes to industry tailwinds) my rating is a Buy and not a Strong Buy.

Upcoming Earnings

Q3 FY25 results are approaching, and the company is still likely to post solid growth, albeit at a lower rate to Q3 FY24's blockbuster 34% revenue growth as large contracts ramped. Around 10% year-over-year revenue growth seems about right for OSI in Q3 FY25. Most importantly, investors will scrutinize the order pipeline. Any update on backlog (which was >$1.8 billion last quarter) will be key. If it stays flat or increases, it signals strong ongoing demand. Look for whether management are seeing continued international opportunities or any slowdown in government decision-making. Moreover, make sure the book-to-bill ratio is over 1. These factors could set the stage positively for the rest of FY25 and the beginning of FY26. Historically, Q4 is OSI's strongest quarter, so any hints from management on how Q4 is shaping up would be valuable. Any signs of order slowdown or margin pressure would likely be met with a quick stock reaction due to the high expectations built into the price.

Conclusion: Buy

OSI Systems presents a compelling investment case due to its strong recent operational performance, record backlog visibility, and strategic positioning within critical global markets. Early signs of improvement in Healthcare and steady contributions from Optoelectronics add valuable diversification, although these segments are more vulnerable at the moment. The Security division is really where I expect the company to continue to shine as geopolitical conditions necessitate higher global security measures. However, investors need to be aware that the stock price is already elevated, and any downward revisions in the forward outlook from management in the coming quarters means that sentiment for the stock could quickly turn negative. Thankfully, management has expressed confidence in navigating tariff-related challenges due to its geographically diverse manufacturing footprint, but margin pressures in the short to medium term still remain a possibility. My 12-month price target of $225 suggests a significant 28.5% upside from the current level of $175, but risks related to cyclical demand and dependency on government spending limit my conviction to a Buy rather than a Strong Buy.
